food_name: Starbucks VIA Tea Essence Hojicha Latte (Instant Stick Pack)

food_subgroup: Instant tea latte powder / stick pack

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for instant hojicha latte stick packs. FOODON_03301099 (tea beverage) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Tea and tea-based beverages) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for instant hojicha latte powder.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 74242007 (Tea substance) is the closest parent; no specific concept exists for hojicha latte instant powder.

preparation_method: dissolved in hot water or milk
allergens: milk (non-dairy creamer may contain milk derivatives — check label)

serving.description: 1 stick pack (approx. 22 g) dissolved in 180–200 ml hot water
serving.natural_language_portion: One stick pack (approximately 22 g of powder) dissolved in 180–200 ml of hot water or steamed milk constitutes a single café-style hojicha latte serving.

nutrient_source.title: Starbucks VIA Tea Essence Hojicha — Nutrition Information (Starbucks Japan / Starbucks Singapore product label data cross-referenced with USDA Branded Food entry for comparable instant hojicha latte powder)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central — Branded Foods; Starbucks product label (Singapore retail)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Starbucks VIA Tea Essence Hojicha sold in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA Branded Food entries for comparable instant hojicha latte powder products (e.g., Keurig Dr Pepper / Ito En branded instant hojicha latte, USDA FDC NDB entries) and cross-referenced with the declared per-serving nutrition panel typical of Starbucks VIA stick packs (approx. 22 g per stick, 5 sticks per pack, SGD 10.70). Per-100 g values are derived by scaling from per-22 g serving data. Values are estimates; the actual product label should be consulted for clinical use.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on sweetened instant tea latte powder with sugar and maltodextrin
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Starbucks VIA Tea Essence Hojicha. GI is estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous instant sweetened tea latte powders containing sugar and maltodextrin as primary carbohydrate sources. Maltodextrin has a GI of approximately 85–105 and sucrose approximately 65; the blended matrix with tea solids and non-dairy creamer moderates the effective GI. An estimate of 55 is conservative and defensible per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 17.1) / 100 ≈ 9.4, where carbohydrate per 22 g serving ≈ 17.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~180 mg per 100 g; ~40 mg per stick serving) and added sugar load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one stick per occasion and avoid adding extra salt or high-sodium milk alternatives, as cumulative sodium from multiple servings may contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (~55 g per 100 g; ~12 g per stick serving) and low glycemic index (~55)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat this product as an occasional indulgence; the approximately 12 g of sugar per stick pack contributes to postprandial glucose excursions and should be counted within the daily carbohydrate budget, ideally consumed alongside a meal to blunt glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from non-dairy creamer (~6 g per 100 g; ~1.3 g per serving) and high added sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit this product to occasional use, as the combination of saturated fat from non-dairy creamer and high added sugar content may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Starbucks VIA Tea Essence Hojicha Latte is an ultra-processed instant beverage sold in Singapore as a retail pack of five single-serve stick packs (SGD 10.70 per pack), available through field survey from Starbucks outlets such as One Fullerton. Each stick pack contains approximately 22 g of instant hojicha latte powder designed to be dissolved in 180–200 ml of hot water or steamed milk to recreate a café-style hojicha tea latte at home or in the office. Hojicha is a Japanese roasted green tea made from bancha or kukicha leaves and stems that have been roasted at high temperature, producing a characteristically warm, toasty, caramel-like flavour with low bitterness and low caffeine compared to standard green tea. The instant powder formulation combines hojicha tea extract or powder with sugar, non-dairy creamer (containing vegetable fat and milk solids), and maltodextrin as a carrier, resulting in a sweet, creamy, lightly roasted tea beverage. Per 100 g of powder, the product delivers approximately 386 kcal, 77 g of carbohydrate (of which ~55 g is sugar), 7 g of fat (predominantly saturated from the creamer), 4.5 g of protein, 1.5 g of dietary fibre, and 180 mg of sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low) based on the sugar and maltodextrin carbohydrate matrix. Clinically, the high sugar content makes this product a moderate concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease, and the sodium content warrants attention for those managing hypertension. The product is suitable for vegetarians but is not vegan due to milk derivatives in the creamer. It is classified as ultra-processed (NOVA Group 4) due to the use of industrial ingredients including maltodextrin, non-dairy creamer, and added flavours.

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore entry exists for Starbucks VIA Tea Essence Hojicha. Nutrient values are estimated from comparable branded instant hojicha latte powder products in USDA Branded Foods and cross-referenced with typical Starbucks VIA stick pack nutrition panel declarations. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the product category (instant sweetened tea latte powder) is well-characterised and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of dry instant powder, not per prepared beverage. The prepared beverage (22 g powder in ~190 ml water) would have substantially lower per-100 ml nutrient density. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this product category. 3. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this product category. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero per typical Starbucks VIA product label declarations — contains no trans fat. 5. potassium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 6. calcium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 7. iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 8. cholesterol_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero as the product uses non-dairy creamer with vegetable fat — contains no cholesterol. 9. vitamin_c_mg through selenium_mcg are all -1 as micronutrient data is not reported in the adapted source for this ultra-processed instant powder. 10. water_g is -1 as this is a dry powder product; water content of the powder itself is not reported in the adapted source. 11. glycemic_index set to 55 (low) as an analogous estimate based on the sugar and maltodextrin carbohydrate matrix per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. 12. The cuisine classification is Japanese because hojicha is a Japanese tea tradition; however, this is a Starbucks commercial product sold globally. 13. Halal and kosher status are unconfirmed; the product label should be consulted. 14. Allergen information is indicative; the actual product label must be consulted for clinical or dietary management purposes. 15. This product is sold as a retail pack of 5 sticks at SGD 10.70 via field survey data (Starbucks One Fullerton).
